wasted_votes_almost_hint: Parties between 2% and 4% — close to the threshold but excluded.
wasted_votes_fringe_hint: Parties below 2% — broad disagreement with all viable options.

wasted_votes_drill_section: Sort polling stations by wasted-vote share

wasted_votes_regions_full: All regions ranked by wasted vote
persistence_description: Share of named-party voters who stayed with the same party across two consecutive elections. Isolates party loyalty from turnout volatility.
persistence_stay_rate_hint: Share of voters who chose the same named party in both elections, out of those who voted for an identifiable party in both.
persistence_churn_hint: Share of named-party voters who switched parties between the two elections.
persistence_top_defection_hint: The single largest party-to-party switch — source party, destination, and share of the source party's outflow.
persistence_tap_region_hint: Tap a region on the map to see its details.
persistence_methodology_body: Counts voters whose named-party choice persisted across cycles. Excludes abstainers, newly registered voters, and the small-parties bucket — those would conflate turnout swings with party loyalty.
persistence_footer: Persistence is estimated from per-section voter flow (NNLS Goodman regression + RAS scaling). The party-loyalty signal is robust at the regional level but is an aggregate estimate, not an individual-voter measurement.
benford_description: First-digit and second-digit distributions of per-section vote counts, by party. Compares observed digit shares against the Benford-expected curve.
benford_caveat_title: This is not evidence of fraud
benford_caveat_body: Section-level vote counts are range-bounded, which violates one of Benford's preconditions (numbers spanning multiple orders of magnitude). Plenty of clean electoral data fails the 1st-digit test. Mebane's election-forensics literature explicitly recommends the second-digit test (2BL) over the first-digit test for vote counts. Treat any deviation here as a curiosity prompting further inspection, not a verdict.
benford_mode_first_hint: Classical Benford test on the leading digit. Range-bounded data often fails this test.
benford_mode_second_hint: Mebane's recommended test for election vote counts. Requires sections with at least 10 votes for the party (so the number has a second digit).
benford_mad_caption: Mean absolute deviation in share space — lower is closer to Benford.
benford_chi2_caption: Chi-squared statistic — scales with n, so compare with caution across parties.
benford_p_caption: Approximate upper-tail p-value; values near 0 simply mean "statistically different from Benford", not "fraudulent".
benford_interpretation: How to read this chart: bars are the share of sections whose vote count for this party starts (or has as second digit) with each digit; the line is the Benford-expected share for that digit. A flat bar pattern or a heavy lean toward 1–2 typically signals that the party's per-section counts are small and range-bounded, not that anything is wrong.
benford_footer: Section-level vote counts per party. Sections below the minimum vote threshold are excluded — they cannot inform either test.
benford_bucket_moderate: Moderate deviation — common for parties with mid-size per-section counts.
benford_bucket_strong: Pronounced deviation — usually a sign of small or range-bounded per-section counts, not fraud.
benford_methodology_description: Why this test exists, what its results actually tell us, and when deviations should not mislead us.
benford_methodology_intro: Benford's law describes a surprising regularity: in many naturally-occurring datasets, the leading digit doesn't appear with equal frequency. The digit '1' shows up in about 30% of values, '2' in 18%, and '9' in only 4–5%. The phenomenon appears in city populations, river lengths, accounting ledgers — anywhere numbers span several orders of magnitude. Election forensics borrowed it on the hypothesis that fabricated results would struggle to follow the same curve. Reality is more complicated, and this page is devoted to those nuances.
benford_methodology_h_2bl: Why the test is for the second digit, not the first
benford_methodology_p_2bl1: The classical Benford test analyses the leading (first) digit. It applies when values vary considerably — for example single-digit numbers mixed with three- and four-digit ones. Per-section results don't behave that way. A standard Bulgarian polling station has 800–1000 registered voters. A party with 5–15% support collects votes that vary in a narrow range. That alone is enough for the first-digit test to 'fail' with no hint of manipulation anywhere.
benford_methodology_p_2bl2: Walter Mebane, one of the leading experts on the topic, demonstrates that the second digit is distributed much more uniformly and depends less on the magnitude of the number. The expected second-digit distribution (the '2BL' test) is nearly flat — '0' appears in about 12% of cases, '9' in about 8.5%. This method works on numbers that don't span several orders of magnitude. For that reason the site defaults to 2BL, while the classical first-digit test remains available via a toggle.
benford_methodology_p_thresholds1: We apply two main filters: a minimum number of votes for a party in a given section, and a minimum number of sections that pass that threshold. The second-digit test needs a number with at least two digits — under 10 votes there is simply no second digit to measure. Beyond that, the literature does not impose a high threshold for the votes per section, since the point of 2BL is precisely to work with smaller values. So our threshold is fixed at 10 votes per section, the same for both tests.
benford_methodology_p_thresholds2: Before any result is computed, we require at least 30 sections that pass the threshold. For smaller samples the chi-squared coefficient becomes unstable and MAD becomes too 'noisy'. Parties below this threshold are not displayed, to avoid misleading conclusions.
benford_methodology_h_metrics: How to read MAD and χ²
benford_methodology_p_metrics1: Two values appear under each chart:
benford_methodology_p_metrics_mad: MAD (mean absolute deviation): the average difference between the observed and Benford-expected shares. It does not depend on the number of sections, which makes it suitable for comparison across parties.
benford_methodology_p_metrics_chi: χ² (chi-squared): this value grows proportionally to n (the number of sections). Larger parties will have a higher χ² even if their distribution is identical to a smaller party's.
benford_methodology_p_metrics2: Based on the MAD value, we sort the results into three buckets:
benford_methodology_p_metrics_close: Close to Benford (MAD < 0.04) — the distribution tracks the curve within statistical noise.
benford_methodology_p_metrics_moderate: Moderate deviation (0.04 ≤ MAD < 0.08) — visible difference, often seen in parties whose result is concentrated in a narrow range.
benford_methodology_p_metrics_strong: Strong deviation (MAD ≥ 0.08) — pronounced difference, almost always driven by a specific scale of the data rather than misconduct.
benford_methodology_h_meaning: What the test shows and what it does NOT show
benford_methodology_p_meaning1: Strong deviation from Benford is almost never proof of manipulation. Most often it is an indication of a party with geographically concentrated support, or of results that cluster around specific values. In either case, 'clean' data can produce a bad Benford result.
benford_methodology_p_meaning2: The reverse holds too: a perfect Benford distribution is no certificate of honesty. Manipulation aimed at only a handful of sections, or one that scales the numbers by a constant factor, will not change the curve. Benford is a 'coarse filter' — useful for noticing that one party's data structurally differs from the rest, but not sufficient for final conclusions.
benford_methodology_h_alt: Where to look for real signals
benford_methodology_p_alt1: Three other tools on the site provide much more reliable signals than Benford:
benford_methodology_p_alt_risk: Risk screening: combines turnout outliers, unusual share concentration, machine-vs-paper divergence, and shifts during protocol re-entry.
benford_methodology_p_alt_problem: Problem sections: focuses on sections in higher-risk areas where one party's result departs sharply from the surrounding sections' average.
benford_methodology_p_alt_suspicious: Suspicious sections: sections with large gaps between machine and paper voting, or where final numbers differ substantially from the preliminary count.
benford_methodology_p_refs_mebane: Mebane, W. R. (2006). Election Forensics: Vote Counts and Benford's Law. Working paper, University of Michigan.
benford_methodology_p_refs_pericchi: Pericchi, L. & Torres, D. (2011). Quick Anomaly Detection by the Newcomb–Benford Law. Statistical Science, 26(4), 502–516.
benford_methodology_p_refs_klimek: Klimek, P., Yegorov, Y., Hanel, R. & Thurner, S. (2012). Statistical detection of systematic election irregularities. PNAS, 109(41), 16469–16473.
benford_methodology_p_refs_beber: Beber, B. & Scacco, A. (2012). What the Numbers Say: A Digit-Based Test for Election Fraud. Political Analysis, 20(2), 211–234.
benford_methodology_p_refs_nigrini: Nigrini, M. (2012). Benford's Law: Applications for Forensic Accounting, Auditing, and Fraud Detection. Wiley.
risk_score_description: Composite 0–100 score per polling section, combining seven independent signals already published as standalone reports: recount delta, flash-memory mismatch, invalid-ballot share, additional voters, single-party concentration, statistical outlier vs. settlement peers, and shift vs. the previous election.
risk_score_caveat_title: This is a screening tool, not a fraud determination
risk_score_caveat_body: A high score means the section is statistically unusual along multiple dimensions and warrants a closer look — that is all. Each underlying signal has innocent explanations (small section size, demographic homogeneity, late voter registration drives, recount procedure correctly catching errors). Always check the per-section decomposition before drawing conclusions.
risk_methodology_description: How the risk score is computed: signals, weights, handling of missing data, categories, and how to interpret results.
risk_methodology_caveat_title: This is an analysis tool, not a verdict
risk_methodology_intro: This page describes the methodology for computing the risk score and — more importantly — what it cannot prove. The tool uses data we already publish in our individual reports. Combining them into a single index is a means of prioritizing review, not new evidence of wrongdoing.
risk_methodology_p_what1: Each polling section receives an index from 0 to 100 that summarizes how statistically atypical it is across seven independent criteria. The output is a ranked list of sections, prioritized for review.
risk_methodology_p_what2: It is not a fraud detector, nor a court-admissible irregularity indicator. The system cannot distinguish an administrative error from intentional action — every signal has its possible innocent explanations.
risk_methodology_p_signals_intro: Each signal is a normalized value (0 to 1) drawn from a specific per-section report. If section data for a particular report is missing, the signal stays "undefined" (not zero) and is handled via a special formula.
risk_methodology_signal_recount_p: Measures the magnitude of changes during the official recount: (added + removed votes) / total votes. Captures vote movement even when the net is zero.
risk_methodology_signal_suemg_p: Difference between the machine's flash-memory record (SUEMG) and the final protocol. Can be caused by technical errors during transfer or by recount adjustments not reflected in both records.
risk_methodology_signal_invalid_p: Share of paper ballots ruled invalid. Normalized with a 30% cap (values above that level are taken as 1.0).
risk_methodology_signal_additional_p: Share of voters added "under the line" on election day. Can be a sign of last-minute mobilization in small settlements, institutional voting, or coordinated pressure.
risk_methodology_signal_concentrated_p: Triggers when the winner in the section takes more than 80% of the vote. Below this threshold the signal is 0.
risk_methodology_signal_peer_p: Compares the section's turnout and winner share against neighboring sections in the same settlement (EKATTE). Captures sections that stand out sharply from their immediate surroundings.
risk_methodology_signal_swing_p: Compares the section's turnout and winner share against the same section at the previous election. Captures the "corporate vote" fingerprint — a section that previously split its vote now delivering a lopsided result at elevated turnout. Ratios are compared rather than absolute counts so the signal is robust to section renumbering. Only an upward shift relative to the national norm is flagged; a decline is not.
risk_methodology_p_formula1: The score is a weighted mean, not a simple sum. The denominator includes only the weights of the signals that fired:
risk_methodology_p_formula2: Where n_i is the value of the signal and w_i is its weight. This way, if data for some indicators is missing for a section (e.g. no machine voting), the score is not artificially deflated.
risk_methodology_p_bands_intro: The number is a guide, but in the interface we use four main categories:
risk_methodology_band_low: No notable deviations. Most sections fall here.
risk_methodology_band_elevated: Some signals worth attention, but they can be coincidental.
risk_methodology_band_critical: Highly unusual data across multiple signals. A mandatory protocol review is recommended.
risk_methodology_important_percentile_p: Since high deviations are demographically normal in some municipalities (e.g. small mountain villages), the percentile helps determine whether a score is high only for that municipality or for the country as a whole.
risk_methodology_important_party_p: This column shows the section's winning party — same convention as every other per-section report. It is NOT the party "affected" by the risk. For specific information on which party gained or lost votes during a recount or flash-memory adjustment, see the per-section breakdown.
risk_methodology_important_evidence_p: A score of 85 based on 5 of 7 signals is much more reliable than the same score based on only 2 signals. Always check the count of available signals (e.g. 3/7).
risk_methodology_important_context_p: The badge for specific neighborhoods is informational only and does not affect the mathematical computation of risk. It helps the reader judge whether the demographic factor explains high concentration.
risk_methodology_p_how_to_use1: Start with the category, not the raw number. Check the count of available signals: scores backed by fewer than 3 signals are analysis-grade only and should not be used as evidence individually.
risk_methodology_p_how_to_use2: Open the per-section breakdown before quoting a score. The waterfall shows which signals fired and at what magnitude — without this context the number is meaningless. A score of 75 built from "all seven signals slightly elevated" is a very different story from a score of 75 built from "recount adjustment +35, everything else zero."
risk_percentile_caption: Higher = more unusual relative to peer sections in the same municipality.
risk_partial_signals_hint: Some signals couldn't be computed — score reflects only the available ones.
risk_full_signals_hint: Score uses every signal — most reliable measurement.
risk_neighborhood_flag: Section sits in a flagged Roma neighborhood (context only — not part of the score).
risk_interpretation: Each signal is normalized to 0–1, multiplied by its weight, then summed. Missing signals are excluded from both numerator and denominator so a thin-data score isn't artificially deflated — but watch the signals-available count: a high score from 2 of 7 signals is far less reliable than a high score from 5 of 7.
risk_footer: Composite of seven independent signals — recount, flash-memory, invalid ballots, additional voters, concentration, peer outlier, shift vs. previous election.
risk_score_not_found: Section not found in the risk-screening report for this election.

about_p_1: Between 2021 and 2026, Bulgaria held an unprecedented seven parliamentary elections without producing a stable, lasting government. The political churn shaped public life and put the country's reforms on hold.
about_p_2: The snap election of 19 April 2026 broke that cycle. A clear parliamentary majority emerged, a new government was sworn in, and for the first time in years a full term in office looks plausible. Whether that stability holds — and whether it translates into policy — is the open question of this political moment.
about_p_3: The years of churn had a real cost. Voter fatigue and disillusionment with politicians created an environment where radical voices, aided by Moscow's disinformation campaigns, gained ground at the expense of the democratic process. Bulgaria slipped behind on EU recovery funds, on the eurozone timeline, and on Schengen integration.
about_p_4: That environment bred conspiracy theories and a corrosive distrust — not just of the electoral system, but of public institutions as a whole. This platform exists to counter that with evidence. It started with elections, and has grown into a single place to follow how Bulgaria is actually governed: the parliament and the people in it, the state budget and where public money goes, public procurement and EU funds, local councils and taxes, and the everyday cost of living — all drawn from open and official sources, free to explore and download.
project_about: This project began as a collaborative, open-source effort between a father and son, welcoming contributors from all backgrounds. What started as a tool for exploring two decades of election results has grown into a broader civic-data platform — and it stays true to the same goal: making public information easy for anyone to find, understand, and use.

mp_profiles_source: Photos and biographies of National Assembly members
rollcall_votes_source: Roll-call vote CSVs from stenogram attachments (powers loyalty, attendance, group cohesion, and the voting-twins analytics)
mp_declarations_source: Property and interest declarations from the Court of Audit (register.cacbg.bg) — MPs, cabinet members, deputy ministers, state-agency heads, regional governors, mayors and municipal councillors
bgpost_postcodes_source: Bulgarian Post postal-code register (data.egov.bg, CC0) — joined to the NSI settlements catalog to resolve the free-text 'registered office' field on cacbg.bg declarations to a specific settlement, so the 'Companies HQ'd here' tile on each settlement page can list MP-linked firms by their headquarters address. Sofia city (postal code 1000…1799) collapses to a single capital entry — no rayon split, as no public dataset cleanly maps Sofia postcodes to the 24 administrative rayons.
local_government_roster_source: Roster of mayors, deputy mayors, council chairs, councillors and chief architects per municipality — derived from the Court of Audit declarations and surfaced on every settlement page
local_taxes_ipi_source: Institute for Market Economics — 265 municipalities: annual FOI-collected survey of municipal tax rates. Covers property tax (legal entities), property-transfer tax, vehicle tax (74–110 kW), retail patent tax (≤100 m²) and taxi patent tax — all 265 municipalities, 2021–2025. Powers the "Local taxes" tile on My Area.
local_taxes_regional_profiles_source: regionalprofiles.bg — analytical companion to the IME dataset, with interactive maps and 2012–2026 comparisons.
local_taxes_naredba_source: Per-municipality bylaws on local taxes and fees (the obshtini.bg platform hosts ~150 of them; the rest publish PDFs on their own sites). Source for residential garbage fee with its basis flag (promille / users / floor area / waste volume), property tax for individuals (where the TAX bylaw is reachable), tourist tax and dog tax — slots IME does not cover.
council_minutes_source: Council resolutions, aggregate vote tallies (за/против/въздържал) and where available the per-councillor named-vote breakdown — scraped from each municipality's own общински-съвет website (protocol PDFs and per-decision extracts). Bulgaria has no central register, so this fans out across heterogeneous CMSes per município. Sixteen municipalities are wired: Sofia, Plovdiv, Varna, Burgas, Stara Zagora, Pleven, Ruse, Велико Търново, Sliven, Габрово, Казанлък, Хасково, Добрич, Димитровград, Разград, and Перник (Габрово, Казанлък, Хасково and Добрич added via Wayback CDX discovery; Димитровград, Разград, and Перник added from clean live WordPress / Joomla indexes — Перник is a tier-A per-councillor source with 28 councillors over 158 resolutions joined at ~82% to the cacbg roster); Blagoevgrad is deferred (legacy URL dead, município migrated to a SaaS that requires auth). Per-councillor named-vote breakdowns are live for Велико Търново (~81%), Burgas (~94% across 86 sessions, pulled from a parallel /video archive that links the full session protokol PDF) and Sofia (~89% across 75 sessions, recovered via Gemini Vision OCR of the full-protocol PDFs since they ship as Cyrillic-to-Latin mojibake). These surface in the My-Area "How the council voted" tile with one mini-avatar per councillor coloured by vote. Tally + adopted/rejected ship for Велико Търново, Stara Zagora, Ruse, Pleven, Burgas and Sofia; the others (Plovdiv, Varna, Sliven) publish in ПРЕПИС-ИЗВЛЕЧЕНИЕ format which carries decision text but no vote counts.

polls_description: How accurate have the major Bulgarian polling agencies been? Comparing their final pre-election polls against the actual results from the Central Election Commission, with party-level bias and ideological-bloc lean.
polls_leaderboard_hint: Mean absolute error in percentage points across each agency's final pre-election polls. Lower is better.
polls_public_money_hint: Open this agency's Commerce Registry entity — its public procurement contracts and EU-fund projects. Several polling agencies also earn money from the state.
polls_election_accuracy_hint: Each agency's last poll before the election, compared to the actual result.
polls_data_source: Source: BG Wikipedia polling tables · Actual results: Central Election Commission · Analysis: Claude Opus 4.7
polls_no_data_for_election: No agency polling data on file for the {{date}} election. The cross-election analysis below still applies.
polls_no_polls_for_agency: No polls on file for this agency yet.
polls_no_detail_breakdown: No party breakdown on file for this poll.
polls_actual_marker_legend: The dark vertical line on each bar marks the actual election result.
polls_section_all_time_hint: Aggregated across every election cycle in our dataset, not just the one selected above.
dashboard_polls_hint: Each agency's final pre-election poll vs. the actual result for this election. MAE = mean absolute error in percentage points across parties.
dashboard_polls_mae_hint: Mean absolute error of this agency's final pre-election poll for this election, in percentage points across parties. Lower is better. Differs from the corrected MAE on the polls page, which pools every election and applies Bayesian shrinkage.
dashboard_polls_days_before_hint: Days between the poll's fieldwork end and election day. Polls farther from the vote tend to score worse — that's a recency artefact, not a methodological one.
dashboard_polls_biggest_miss_hint: The party where this agency's final poll was most off. Signed difference vs. the actual result in percentage points — positive means the agency overestimated, negative means it underestimated.
dashboard_accuracy_trends_hint: Cross-agency average mean absolute error (MAE) per election. Bars above the dashed line are elections where polls were collectively further off than usual — often a signal that something unexpected happened (a late shift, a new party, or a sampling miss).
polls_anomalous_hint: When every agency misses by more than 3pp, it usually points to a late shift in voter sentiment or a party that was hard to sample.
polls_methodology_intro: Each agency's score is based on the mean absolute error (MAE) in percentage points across named parties. It is computed by comparing the agency's last pre-election poll to the official CEC result — the smaller the error, the more accurate the poll.
polls_methodology_section1_body: Polls that explicitly publish an undecided share are normalised before scoring. The undecided share is redistributed proportionally across the named parties so the sum matches the official result's denominator (based only on those who voted).
polls_methodology_section1_icon_note: A scale icon next to an MAE value indicates that proportional redistribution was applied to that poll.
polls_methodology_section1_genre_note: If the agency publishes a finished forecast (rather than raw attitudes), the values are scored as-is to avoid double-counting the undecided share.
polls_methodology_section2_body: To prevent a single lucky cycle from topping the table, the leaderboard uses corrected MAE. An agency's per-election errors are pooled across every election it covered and pulled toward the cross-agency mean via Bayesian shrinkage.
polls_methodology_section2_formula: (n × agency_MAE + k × overall_mean_MAE) / (n + k), with n = elections covered and k = 4
polls_methodology_section2_logic_note: An agency with 1 election weights only 1/5 to its own number and 4/5 to the cross-agency mean. An 8-cycle agency weights 8/12 to itself and 4/12 to the mean.
polls_methodology_section3_body: The final grade is a composite score based on three components:
polls_methodology_section3_signal2: Plus/Minus vs. consensus: rewards agencies (up to ±0.5) that successfully forecast deviations from the cross-agency trend.
polls_methodology_section3_signal3: 4%-barrier accuracy: rewards correct over/under-threshold placements via a 1.5× multiplier.
polls_methodology_caveat: Polls farther from the vote (larger "days before") tend to score worse. That's typically a recency artefact (sentiment shifts in the final days), not necessarily a methodological flaw in the agency itself.
polls_normalization_applied: Normalisation applied: {{pp}}pp of undecided share redistributed across the named parties before scoring.
polls_median_days_before_hint: Median number of days between fieldwork end and election day across this agency's scored polls. Lower means the agency typically polls right before the vote — a structural accuracy advantage. A high number signals stale polls dragging the score down.
polls_grade_hint: Composite letter grade A+ through F derived from shrunk MAE, Plus/Minus, and barrier-call accuracy. Tunable thresholds; see methodology.
polls_shrunk_mae_hint: Sample-weighted, industry-bias-subtracted MAE put through Bayesian shrinkage. For each (election, party) the cross-agency mean error is subtracted from every agency's error before |·| — so cycle-wide forecast shocks (a new party absorbing late-deciders, an industry-wide turnout-model miss) aren't charged to individual agencies. Each per-poll contribution is then weighted by √n. Same Bayesian shrinkage as before applies on top: (n × MAE_adj + k × overall_mean) / (n + k), k=4. Reads as 'skill relative to peers', not absolute error.
polls_plus_minus_hint: Average difference between this agency's MAE and the consensus MAE of all other agencies on the same election. Positive means the agency consistently beat the field; negative means it consistently lagged.
polls_barrier_call_hint: Share of (party, election) pairs where the agency's last poll correctly placed the party above or below the 4% parliamentary barrier.

vote_flow_methodology_description: How the "where the votes went" estimates are computed and what they can and cannot tell you.
vote_flow_methodology_intro: Election results tell us how many votes each party received in each section, but not which voters moved where. The Sankey chart reconstructs flows from observed margins using ecological regression — a statistical inference, not a measurement of individuals.
vote_flow_methodology_p_estimate1: For each oblast, we run a <goodman>Goodman-style</goodman> non-negative least-squares regression across the oblast's polling sections. Each section contributes one equation per to-cycle party, fit against the from-cycle party shares as predictors. The fitted coefficients form a transition matrix: the share of party A's voters in cycle T who voted for party B in cycle T+1. Sections are weighted by registered voter count.
vote_flow_methodology_p_estimate2: We then biproportionally scale the matrix (the <ras>RAS algorithm</ras>) so that each row exactly sums to that party's published from-cycle vote total and each column exactly sums to its to-cycle total. Per-oblast matrices are summed in absolute votes to produce the national view.
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo1: Some Sankey nodes don't represent parties — they represent the rest of the electorate so the chart conserves the registered-voter pool:
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_abstain: "Did not vote" — registered voters who didn't cast a valid party vote (includes invalid ballots and "I support no one" votes).
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_joined: "Newly registered" — a service node on the from side. Represents people who entered the voter rolls between the two elections: turned 18, returned from abroad, newly registered. Its size is not estimated — it comes straight from the change in registered-voter counts on the section protocols, computed separately for each electoral district whose rolls grew.
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_exited: "Removed from rolls" — the mirror image, on the to side. Represents people who left the rolls (death, deregistration). Computed the same way, for the districts whose rolls shrank. Within a single district only one of the two nodes appears (the roll change is net — we don't see the separate counts of who entered and who left), but at the national level both are often visible.
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_small: "Other parties" — combined bucket for parties that scored below 1% in both cycles, to keep the chart legible.
vote_flow_methodology_h_scope: Why only at oblast level and above
vote_flow_methodology_p_scope: The estimate works because sections within an oblast voted differently from one another — those differences are exactly what lets the regression separate out "what fraction of party A's voters went to party B". At finer scopes there simply aren't enough differences to learn from: a typical municipality has 30–50 sections, a settlement often only 1–3, and a single section has none. The math becomes underdetermined — many different transition stories fit the observed totals equally well. That's why on municipality, settlement and section pages we show the raw before-after deltas (Party X: 32% → 28%) instead of inventing a flow estimate.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_coalition: Coalition splits and mergers (PP+DB merger, ITN's collapse, the DPS split into ДПС-НН and АПС) appear as cross-party flows even when they reflect administrative changes more than individual voter movement. Treat such transitions with extra skepticism.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_sections: We match polling sections by ID. About 1–3% of sections per cycle pair have unstable IDs (re-issued, mobile sections, ship sections); those are dropped from the regression and reported as "Sections dropped" in the diagnostics.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_abroad: Abroad sections (oblast 32) are excluded entirely — their section IDs are re-issued every cycle, so a section-level join produces noise rather than signal.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_oblast: Oblast-level estimates have higher uncertainty than the national rollup, especially in small oblasts and on cycle pairs with brand-new parties. The diagnostic residual on each cycle pair gives a sense of how cleanly the math balanced.
vote_flow_methodology_p_trust1: The biggest flows on stable cycle pairs (party self-loops, abstain-to-abstain, well-known migrations between similar parties) are robust — they come out the same under multiple model variants. Small flows between similar-sized parties are noisy and should be read as a rough order of magnitude rather than a precise count.
vote_flow_methodology_p_trust2: Activation and demobilisation flows (party ↔ "Did not vote") are usually the most informative ones, because Bulgarian turnout has swung enormously between cycles. Most of what looks like "voters defecting between parties" is in fact activation/demobilisation in disguise.

source_declarations: Source: property and interest declarations filed with the Bulgarian Court of Audit (Сметна палата). Sitting MPs cannot legally hold management roles, so this list covers ownership stakes only.
dashboard_region_mps_hint: MPs elected from this region for the corresponding National Assembly. The party shown is the one each candidate ran with. Photos and biographies are from parliament.bg. Hover a dot or avatar for details.
dashboard_party_voters_hint: Total votes cast for this party in this election, with delta vs the prior election. The percentage is the party's share of all valid votes nationally.
dashboard_party_position_hint: The party's overall ranking by total votes in this election, with arrow showing change vs the prior election.
dashboard_party_paper_machine_hint: How this party's voters split between paper ballots and voting machines, with the change vs prior election. Useful for spotting demographic shifts in the party's base.
dashboard_party_top_region_hint: The region contributing the most votes to this party. Position is the party's rank within that region's vote.
dashboard_party_top_regions_hint: All electoral regions ranked by votes for this party. Bar = absolute votes; % = party's share of the region's vote; change = percentage-point swing vs prior election. Click a region to drill into its full results.
dashboard_party_top_municipalities_hint: Municipalities where this party drew the most votes. % local = party's share of that municipality's vote; change = absolute vote difference vs prior election.
dashboard_party_top_settlements_hint: Towns and villages where this party drew the most votes. % local = party's share of that settlement's vote; change = absolute vote difference vs prior election.
dashboard_party_swings_hint: Regions where the party gained or lost the most percentage points vs the prior election. Useful for identifying where to defend, attack, or investigate.
dashboard_party_fingerprint_hint: How this party's vote share correlates with each demographic across all 265 municipalities (Pearson r, +1 = perfect alignment, −1 = perfect inverse). Click any row to open the cross-tab scatter for that pair.
dashboard_party_fingerprint_note: Pearson correlation across {{count}} demographic dimensions, computed over all 265 municipalities.
dashboard_demographic_cleavages_hint: How the top parties' municipal vote shares correlate with each demographic dimension across all 265 municipalities. Wider spread between dots = the dimension was a sharper dividing line in this election. Click any row for the cross-tab scatter.
dashboard_demographic_cleavages_note: Each dot is one party that cleared the 4% threshold; horizontal position is the Pearson r between that party's municipal vote share and the demographic.
local_demographic_cleavages_hint: How the leading council parties' vote shares correlate with each demographic dimension across municipalities. Wider spread between dots = the dimension was a sharper dividing line in this cycle.
local_demographic_cleavages_note: Each dot is one of the leading council parties; horizontal position is the Pearson r between its council vote share by municipality and the demographic.
local_mayor_demographic_cleavages_hint: How the leading parties' first-round mayoral vote shares correlate with each demographic dimension across municipalities. Wider spread between dots = the dimension was a sharper dividing line in the mayoral race.
local_mayor_demographic_cleavages_note: Each dot is one of the leading parties; horizontal position is the Pearson r between its first-round mayoral vote share by municipality and the demographic.
dashboard_party_assessment_hint: AI-written analysis of this party's performance — what worked, what didn't, and where to focus next campaign. Generated offline from the party's vote, polling, and (where available) financing data.
dashboard_assessment_not_generated: Retrospect not yet generated for this party. Run the party-retrospect skill to produce one.
dashboard_party_top_candidates_hint: The party's strongest preferential-vote candidates — those whose personal vote count was highest within the party. Click a candidate for the full profile across regions.
dashboard_party_raised_funds_hint: Total funds raised by this party for the campaign — sum of contributions from the party itself, donors, candidates, and the state media package. Delta compares to the same party's total in the prior election.
dashboard_party_campaign_cost_hint: Total declared campaign expenses including non-monetary contributions. A higher cost than the prior cycle is shown in red, lower in green. The secondary line shows how much of the cost came from non-monetary contributions.
dashboard_party_top_expense_hint: The single largest expense bucket on this party's filing — useful as a quick read of where the campaign budget went.
dashboard_party_donors_count_hint: Number of declared individual donors and the total amount they contributed. The candidates line shows the same for candidate self-funding. Click the count to see the full donor list.
dashboard_party_expense_breakdown_hint: All campaign expense categories ranked by amount. Bar = absolute spend; share = percent of total cost; change = percent change vs the prior election (red = increase, green = decrease).
dashboard_party_top_donors_hint: The 10 largest individual donors to this party's campaign. Bar = donor's amount relative to the largest donor; share = percent of all donor contributions.
dashboard_candidate_preferences_hint: Total preferential votes the candidate received across all regions, with the percent change vs the prior election. The secondary share is the candidate's slice of the party's total preferences this election.
dashboard_candidate_paper_machine_hint: How the candidate's preferential votes split between paper ballots and voting machines, with the change vs prior election.
dashboard_candidate_ballot_hint: List position(s) the candidate occupied on the party ballot. Some candidates ran in more than one region; each region has its own list number.
dashboard_candidate_top_region_hint: The region where the candidate drew the most preferential votes. Share is relative to all preferential votes for the party in that region.
dashboard_candidate_regions_hint: Per-region breakdown of preferential votes for this candidate. Bar = absolute votes; % region = candidate's share of all valid votes in that region; change = absolute vote difference vs prior election.
dashboard_candidate_top_settlements_hint: Towns and villages where the candidate drew the most preferential votes. % of party = candidate's share of the party's preferences in that settlement; % local = candidate's share of all valid votes in that settlement.
dashboard_candidate_top_sections_hint: Polling stations where the candidate drew the most preferential votes. % of party = candidate's share of the party's preferences in that station; % local = candidate's share of all valid votes in that station.
dashboard_candidate_trajectory_hint: Preferential votes for this candidate across consecutive elections, broken down by region. Useful for spotting whether the candidate's personal vote is growing or fading.
dashboard_candidate_donations_hint: Self-funded contributions declared by this candidate to their party's campaign. Total combines monetary and non-monetary contributions.

mp_voting_per_session_pending: Per-vote breakdown and dissent deep-links arrive in the next phase, alongside the session pages.
votes_index_description: Every voting day in the Bulgarian National Assembly, with per-MP and per-party breakdowns.
votes_index_intro: Every voting day in the National Assembly. Click a date to see how MPs voted on each item.
votes_index_empty: No roll-call sessions have been ingested yet.
votes_session_not_found: No roll-call data was found for this date.
votes_session_no_party_data: Per-party breakdown unavailable for this item.
cohesion_outlier_title: Most independent and most loyal MPs
mp_similarity_empty: Not enough vote overlap to compute peers.
votes_landing_breaks_empty: No contested votes in the last 7 days.
votes_landing_pair_intro: Items where {{partyA}} and {{partyB}} voted opposite ways
votes_landing_pair_empty: No breaks found between these groups in the current parliament.
votes_heatmap_axis_groups: Parliamentary groups · items sorted by closeness · each cell shows the group's vote split
votes_session_turnout_hint: Share of seated MPs who cast a vote (yes/no/abstain), averaged across items.
votes_session_cohesion_hint: Average share of each group voting the same way, weighted by group size. 100% = every group unanimous.
votes_session_dissents_hint: Number of (MP × item) votes against the MP's own group plurality.
votes_session_closest_hint: Smallest |yes − (no + abstain)| margin among the session's items.
votes_session_dissents_lead: MPs who broke with the group plurality.
companies_hq_screen_lede: {{count}} companies registered here are linked to {{mpCount}} MP(s) — by declared ownership stake or Commerce Registry officer/owner role.
companies_hq_screen_empty: No MP-linked companies found at this address.
cohesion_description: How unified Bulgarian parliamentary groups vote — mean and median cohesion per item across the current parliament.
cohesion_intro: How unified each parliamentary group is when its members vote. Cohesion = share of the group voting the same way per item, averaged across every item the group participated in. 1.00 means every member voted the same way every time; 0.50 is an even split. Absences are excluded.
cohesion_empty: No cohesion data has been computed yet — run the derived-metrics step first.
cohesion_methodology_note: The list aggregates cohesion across every item ingested; the chart shows per-session mean cohesion. Absences are excluded from both.
party_cohesion_hint: Share of group members voting the same way per item, averaged. Absences excluded.
cohesion_chart_y_hint: Y axis: per-session mean cohesion (1.0 = unanimous). Lines connect dates the group voted on at least one item.
similarity_description: Cosine similarity between Bulgarian MPs over their roll-call vote vectors — top peers within and across parliamentary groups.
similarity_intro: Pick an MP to see whose votes most closely match theirs. Cross-party twins — MPs from different parliamentary groups who consistently vote the same way — appear first. Similarity is cosine similarity over yes/no vote vectors; absences are excluded.
similarity_pick_prompt: Choose an MP above to see their voting twins.
similarity_no_data: No similarity data is available for this MP yet.
similarity_cross_party_hint: MPs from other parliamentary groups whose votes most closely match this MP. These signal informal blocs that cross party lines.
similarity_same_party_hint: Top peers within this MP's own parliamentary group, ranked by vote similarity.
embedding_description: 2D UMAP projection of every sitting Bulgarian MP's roll-call vote vector — clusters reveal informal blocs.
embedding_intro: Each dot is one MP. Distance approximates how differently two MPs vote — neighbours vote the same way most of the time. Layout is a UMAP projection of the full vote-vector space, coloured by parliamentary group. Clusters reveal informal blocs.
embedding_empty: No embedding data has been computed yet — run the derived-metrics step first.
embedding_outliers_hint: MPs whose nearest neighbours are mostly from a different parliamentary group — the most plausible candidates for cross-party voting.
embedding_outliers_empty: Every MP's nearest neighbours stay within their own group.
embedding_cross_share_title: Share of nearest voting-neighbours from another parliamentary group
embedding_interaction_hint: Drag to pan · scroll to zoom · or use the controls
mp_twins_intro: Other MPs whose voting record most closely matches this one. Those from a different parliamentary group appear first.
hub_description: Roll-call voting analytics for the Bulgarian National Assembly: archive, group cohesion, attendance, voting twins, and a 2D map of MP voting behaviour.
hub_intro: Roll-call voting data from the Bulgarian National Assembly. Each tile previews one analysis — click into a tile for the full breakdown.
hub_similarity_lede: Largest cross-party overlap: the MP whose voting record best matches MPs from other groups.
hub_cohesion_lede: Average within-group unanimity per parliamentary group. 100% means every member always voted together.
hub_data_note: Source: parliament.bg stenograms. Per-MP, per-item votes are extracted from the official roll-call CSV attached to each plenary day.
hub_votes_desc: Every plenary day with a roll-call vote. Drill into items, tallies, and per-party breakdowns.
hub_cohesion_desc: How unified each parliamentary group votes. Mean, median, and trend over time.
hub_similarity_desc: Cosine similarity between MPs. Cross-party twins surfaced first — the informal blocs that cross party lines.
hub_embedding_desc: 2D UMAP projection of every MP's vote vector. Clusters reveal blocs; bridge MPs reveal cross-party voters.
hub_most_present_lede: MPs with the highest share of roll-call items where they cast a vote (yes / no / abstain).
hub_most_absent_lede: MPs with the highest share of roll-call items where they were recorded as absent.
attendance_description: Per-MP roll-call attendance — share of items where the MP cast a vote versus items where the roll-call recorded them as absent.
attendance_intro: Share of roll-call items where each MP cast a vote (yes / no / abstain). The denominator is items where the MP appears in the roll-call at all, so the metric scopes to each MP's seated window. MPs who have already left the chamber, or who appeared in fewer than 30 items, are excluded.
attendance_empty: No attendance data has been computed yet — run the derived-metrics step first.
attendance_methodology_note: Items counted only when the MP appears in the per-item roll-call. "Absent" is the literal code the CSV records — leave votes, committee work and procedural absences all collapse into one bucket.
dashboard_parliament_voting_desc: How parliamentary groups vote in relation to one another. Cells show cosine similarity of each pair's majority-vote vectors — greener cells mean the two groups vote alike, redder cells mean they vote against each other.
dashboard_parliament_legend_note: Values are % cosine over per-item majority vectors.
connections_no_pairs: No MP↔MP connections match the current filters.
connections_find_intro: Pick any two MPs — we'll find the shortest path between them through declared companies and shared associates.
connections_find_no_path: No path was found between these two MPs through declared companies or shared associates.
connections_find_empty: Pick a starting MP above to begin.
connections_hero_matrix_caption: Party × party MP↔MP ties — click a cell to drill in.
tr_no_current_records: No currently-active officers or owners on file.
ngo_browse_subtitle: Associations, foundations and community centres from the Commerce & NPO register
ngo_signal_politician_long: Politician or official on the governing body
ngo_signal_politician_hint: A politically-exposed person (PEP) of this name sits on the board — a risk category matched by name, not an allegation.
ngo_signal_magistrate_hint: A magistrate of this name appears on the board — a possible name match, a trace, not proof.
ngo_signal_related_party_long: A board member controls a public-contractor firm
ngo_signal_related_party_hint: A person on the board controls a company that wins public procurement — a potential conflict of interest, a trace, not proof.
ngo_signal_contracts_hint: The organisation is a contractor on public-procurement contracts.
ngo_signal_single_bid_hint: Much of the won value is on procedures with a single submitted bid.
ngo_signal_eu_funds_hint: The organisation runs projects financed by the EU funds.
ngo_signal_subsidy_hint: The organisation receives a subsidy from the state budget.
ngo_signal_foreign_hint: An informational indicator — an absolute amount from foreign donors, not a rating.
ngo_signal_large_hint: Total public money (contracts + EU + subsidies) exceeds EUR 1M.
tr_source_commerce_registry: Source: Bulgarian Commerce Registry (Търговски регистър) open data on data.egov.bg. Roles include both currently held and historical ones, since active management while seated is restricted by ЗПК Art. 35.
dashboard_governments_hint: Each cabinet since 2005 shown as a coloured band, with annual GDP growth, inflation, and unemployment overlaid. Click "see details" for the full page including governance indices, trust trends, EU funds and OSCE/ODIHR observation reports.
governments_description: Every Bulgarian cabinet since 2005, set against the macroeconomic backdrop of its term.
governments_explainer: Each cabinet's tenure is marked with a coloured band, overlaid with Eurostat data for annual GDP growth, inflation and unemployment. Dashed vertical lines mark parliamentary elections.
indicators_unemployment_monthly_latest: Unemployment — latest month (seasonally adjusted): {{label}} · {{value}}%. The line above is quarterly; the newest completed quarter is the last point plotted.
governments_chart_labour_explainer: Unemployment doesn't tell the whole story. Bulgaria's headline rate (around 3–4%) looks low partly because it excludes people who have left the labour force entirely — through emigration or discouragement. The employment and activity rates (ages 20–64) show the fuller picture, and 'labour market slack' adds the underemployed and the available-but-not-searching.
governments_chart_labour_participation: Employment rate and activity rate (20–64, seasonally adjusted)
indicators_labour_slack: Labour market slack, {{year}}: {{value}}% of the extended labour force — about {{ratio}}× the official unemployment rate ({{unemp}}%). The broad measure adds part-time underemployed and people available to work but outside the official count, so its denominator is wider and the two are not directly comparable.
indicators_labour_slack_short: Labour market slack, {{year}}: {{value}}% of the extended labour force — a broad measure that adds the underemployed and discouraged to the officially unemployed.
governments_chart_governance: Governance: corruption perception and Worldwide Governance Indicators
governments_chart_governance_explainer: Transparency International's CPI scores Bulgaria 0–100 (higher = cleaner). World Bank WGI scores run roughly −2.5 to +2.5 (higher = better). They are on different scales, so toggle them one at a time to read the trend.
governments_chart_cpi_explainer: Annual CPI score for Bulgaria on a 0–100 scale (0 = highly corrupt, 100 = very clean). The Y-axis is zoomed to 30–60 because the score has hovered in the low 40s for the entire period.
governments_chart_wgi_explainer: Three of the six WGI dimensions, all on a roughly −2.5 to +2.5 scale (higher = better). The Y-axis is zoomed to −0.5 to +0.7 to make Bulgaria's movement legible.
governments_chart_trust_explainer: Standard Eurobarometer "tend to trust" share for Bulgarian respondents — annual mean of the spring and autumn waves. Trust in the EU has consistently been higher than in domestic institutions.
governments_chart_fiscal: Fiscal: debt, budget balance, current account
governments_chart_fiscal_explainer: Quarterly Eurostat series, all expressed as % of GDP. Government debt (Maastricht definition) tracks the stock; the budget balance is net lending/borrowing of general government per quarter (negative = deficit); the current account is the external balance with the rest of the world. Quarterly figures swing more than the annual averages each cabinet is judged by.
governments_chart_fiscal_nominal_explain: Same fiscal series in nominal euros rather than as a share of GDP — what each cabinet adds in absolute terms. The stock chart shows the gross debt level at the end of every quarter; the flows chart shows net new debt issued per quarter (the quarter-on-quarter change in the debt stock), the budget balance (negative = deficit financed by new borrowing), and the current account balance with the rest of the world.
governments_chart_fiscal_nominal_flows: Quarterly flows — new debt issued, budget balance, current account
governments_chart_fiscal_reserve_explain: End-of-quarter stock of cash held by the state, in EUR. Not to be confused with the current account above (an external-trade flow that can be any sign) or with BNB's foreign-exchange reserves (which back the currency board). The Law on Public Finance requires the fiscal reserve to stay above a floor set in each year's State Budget Law — currently 4.5 BGN bn ≈ €2.3 bn (dashed line). Sourced from the Ministry of Finance monthly КФП bulletin.
cabinet_budgets_teaser: Which PM / finance-minister duo ran the budget best — balance, overdue obligations and the fiscal reserve, by cabinet.
cabinet_budgets_about_lead: Which Prime-Minister / Finance-Minister duo ran the state budget best?
cabinet_budgets_about_what_b: Each row is one calendar year: the budget balance (deficit or surplus as a share of GDP), the year-end overdue obligations and the fiscal reserve (consolidated central government, social-security funds and municipalities). Years are grouped under the cabinet that governed them longest — shown once, with a party colour and the average balance; transition years also name the other cabinets that shared them. The finance minister who defended (got adopted) each budget and who amended it is noted too — often not the one who executes it, since the budget for year Y is adopted late in the previous year.
cabinet_budgets_about_measures_b: The balance is shown on two bases: accrual, on the Eurostat methodology (ESA 2010, full period), and cash, from the Ministry of Finance consolidated fiscal programme (КФП) — the figure politicians usually quote. The two can diverge materially (2022, for instance, was −0.8% cash vs −3.0% ESA) because the cash measure counts money when it moves while accrual counts it in the period it belongs to. Figures are full-year totals — the data can't split them precisely between cabinets that shared a year. Sorting is chronological by default; click a column header to sort by it.
cabinet_budgets_about_sources_b: The cash balance follows the Ministry of Finance official annual report and currently covers 2014–2024 (earlier years and the current year to follow). Overdue obligations and the fiscal reserve are also from the Ministry. Overdue obligations appear only for ingested years; for 2022 the Ministry's source file carries an obvious error in the municipal arrears (≈ €46.5 bn, mirrored in CEIC). The fiscal reserve's scope was broadened in 2014 (Public Finance Act), so pre-2014 values aren't fully comparable with later ones.
cabinet_budgets_hero_aria: Budget balance by year (% of GDP), coloured by governing party, with cabinet and finance-minister tracks and the EU −3% deficit limit
cabinet_budgets_arrears_pending: Overdue-obligations data has not been ingested yet.
cabinet_budgets_ranking_heading: Which cabinet ran the budget best?
cabinet_budgets_ranking_note: Elected cabinets only — caretakers govern a few months and inherit someone else's fiscal year, so they are not ranked.
governments_chart_government_size: Government revenue, expenditure and inward investment (EUR)
governments_chart_government_size_explai: Quarterly general government revenue and expenditure (seasonally and calendar adjusted) — the vertical gap between them equals the quarterly deficit. Below, two annual context tiles: nominal GDP (Eurostat current prices) and net foreign direct investment received by Bulgaria (BPM6, available from 2013).
fdi_monthly_explainer: Net monthly FDI flows into Bulgaria from the BNB balance-of-payments release (BPM6), since 2010. The bars split each month into equity, reinvested earnings and debt instruments; the line is the net total. The cards above sum the year so far against the same months a year earlier — the figures the euro-adoption coverage cites.
fdi_monthly_latest: Latest reported month: {{period}}. Recent months are preliminary and revised with each release.
debt_emissions_explainer: Per-issuance detail: every meaningful Bulgarian sovereign debt instrument, both international (Eurobonds since 2002) and domestic (BNB auctions of treasury bonds / ДЦК, 2019 onward). For each: principal, coupon rate, weighted-average settlement yield, term and maturity. International entries are hand-curated from Ministry of Finance press releases and Luxembourg Stock Exchange listings. Domestic entries are scraped from the BNB Fiscal Agent auction archive — each row is one auction event (reopenings of the same emission appear as separate rows). Bids-rejected auctions show 0 principal sold and are flagged in notes. ISINs are shown for Eurobonds where verifiable.
governments_chart_inflation_breakdown: Inflation breakdown: food, energy, services, core
governments_chart_inflation_breakdown_ex: Quarterly mean of the monthly HICP year-on-year rates by ECOICOP group. Stacking is illustrative — each component is a separate YoY rate, not weighted by basket share — but it makes the dominant driver of any given period visually obvious (e.g. the energy spike in 2022).
governments_chart_sentiment_explainer: Quarterly means of the monthly EU business and consumer surveys. Consumer confidence is a balance (positive = more optimistic than pessimistic); the Economic Sentiment Indicator is an index where 100 equals the long-term average. Sentiment usually leads the macro turning points by a quarter or two.
governments_chart_social_explainer: Four indicators of how the macro environment lands on households: youth unemployment, year-on-year change in the house price index, the Gini coefficient of disposable income (higher = more unequal), and the at-risk-of-poverty rate (% of population below 60% of median income). House prices and youth unemployment are quarterly; Gini and poverty are annual with ~1-year publication lag.
governments_chart_eu_funds_explainer: Annual EU budget flows for Bulgaria, sourced directly from the European Commission's per-Member-State budget data (TOTAL EXPENDITURE and TOTAL National contributions rows in the BG column). The blue line is total EU spending in Bulgaria (cohesion + CAP + recovery instruments); the orange line is Bulgaria's contribution to the EU budget (VAT, GNI, plastic-packaging-waste own resources). The visual gap between the two equals Bulgaria's net annual benefit. The 2008 marker flags the EU's mid-year freeze of ~€825M in pre-accession funds (PHARE/ISPA/SAPARD) and the permanent loss of ~€220M of PHARE after OLAF investigations — though gross 2008 disbursements still rose YoY because CAP direct payments and pre-financing kept flowing.
governments_observations_explainer: Each parliamentary election since 2005 has been observed by OSCE/ODIHR. The mission reports below summarise the international assessment of campaign conduct, voter-list integrity, and recurring concerns such as vote buying.
observations_description: International election observation mission reports for every Bulgarian parliamentary election since 2005 — findings, recommendations and links to full OSCE/ODIHR reports.
observations_explainer: Every parliamentary election held in Bulgaria since 2005 has been observed by OSCE/ODIHR. The mission reports assess campaign conduct, voter-list integrity, media coverage, campaign-finance transparency, and recurring concerns such as vote buying. Summaries below are generated by Claude AI; click Open report for the full ODIHR publication.
gov_pm_prior_party: Prime Minister's prior party (caretaker, formally non-partisan)
governments_headline_tile_explainer: GDP growth, HICP inflation and unemployment over each cabinet's term. For the full set of fiscal, governance and social indicators, see the indicators page.
indicators_page_description: Bulgaria's macroeconomic, fiscal, governance and social indicators since 2005, set against each cabinet's term.
indicators_page_explainer: Each cabinet's tenure is shown as a coloured band so trends can be read against who was in power. Sources are Eurostat, World Bank Worldwide Governance Indicators, Transparency International CPI and Standard Eurobarometer.
indicators_compare_toggle_on_tooltip: Turn on peer overlay — adds RO, GR, HU and HR lines to every chart on this page (EU27 is always shown as a dashed reference).
indicators_compare_toggle_off_tooltip: Turn off the four peer-country lines. EU27 reference stays.
indicators_economy_description: Bulgaria's growth, inflation and labour-market indicators since 2005, set against each cabinet's term.
indicators_fiscal_description: Public-finance indicators: debt, balance, reserve, government size, debt issuances and EU-funds flows since 2005.
indicators_governance_description: Corruption perceptions, World Bank governance scores and Eurobarometer trust in institutions.
indicators_society_description: Youth unemployment, house prices, income inequality, at-risk-of-poverty rate, and criminal-justice indicators.
indicators_society_digital_explainer: The share of people with at least basic digital skills on the DigComp framework — the human capital behind the digital economy and e-government. Bulgaria is among the last in the EU, and its young people (16-24) rank last. The same data drives the companion tile on /sector/administration.
indicators_society_safety_explainer: Two cross-EU comparable indicators from Eurostat. Intentional homicide rate (ICCS0101) is the most universally defined offence category — lower is better. Prison population per 100,000 inhabitants reflects both crime levels and how punitively the system responds, so direction is ambiguous. Toggle "Compare with EU peers" to see Bulgaria against the EU27 mean (computed from members), Romania, Greece, Hungary and Croatia.
eu_compare_page_description: Bulgaria side-by-side with EU27 and four neighbour/CEE peers (Romania, Greece, Hungary, Croatia) — governance, budget, income, longevity.
eu_compare_cabinet_anchor_label: Pick a cabinet to see the data at the end of its term
eu_compare_callout_header_finished: Panels below show values at the end of:
eu_compare_callout_header_incumbent: Panels below show the latest available values (cabinet still in office):
eu_compare_callout_no_selection: No cabinet picked — panels below show values at the selected election.
eu_compare_section_wgi_explainer: The World Bank's six criteria for quality of governance: voice & accountability, political stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, and control of corruption. Countries are scored from −2.5 to +2.5, with 0 the world mean. Bulgaria has consistently trailed the EU average on all six — the chart shows where the gap is widest.
eu_compare_section_snapshot_explainer: The table shows the most recent value for each indicator for Bulgaria and the visible peers, along with Bulgaria's rank in the EU27 where the direction is unambiguous. Cells are coloured against the EU27 average according to each indicator's logic: lower-is-better for inflation, debt and unemployment; higher-is-better for GDP growth and budget balance.
eu_compare_section_snapshot_footnote: Source: Eurostat. Peer values may lag Bulgaria by up to 4 quarters when the peer's series publishes later — hover a cell to see the period offset.
eu_compare_section_cofog_explainer: General-government expenditure broken out by function, as % of GDP. The full bar height shows the share of government spending in the economy — countries with larger budgets stand taller. The coloured segments show what the money goes toward. Below the chart: the largest gaps between Bulgaria and the EU27 average.
eu_compare_cofog_delta_less: Bulgaria spends {{pp}} pp less of GDP on {{function}} than the EU27 average (BG {{bg}}% vs EU {{eu}}%).
eu_compare_cofog_delta_more: Bulgaria spends {{pp}} pp more of GDP on {{function}} than the EU27 average (BG {{bg}}% vs EU {{eu}}%).
eu_compare_cofog_footnote: Source: Eurostat (gov_10a_exp), {{year}}. Greece does not report a COFOG functional breakdown to Eurostat, so it is omitted from this chart.
eu_compare_section_inequality_explainer: Three key SILC indicators (Statistics on Income and Living Conditions) of inequality and poverty: the Gini coefficient (0 = perfect equality, 100 = perfect inequality), the S80/S20 ratio (income share of the top 20% relative to the bottom 20%), and the AROPE index (share of population at risk of poverty or social exclusion). For all three, a lower number is the better result. Unfortunately, Bulgaria ranks last (27/27) in the EU on all three.
eu_compare_section_scatters_explainer: Is the money worth it? Each scatter pairs government spending (% of GDP) against the resulting outcome for the same peer set. Dashed lines mark the EU27 average and split the chart into four zones: top-left (the ideal corner) — high outcomes at low spend, money is being managed efficiently; bottom-right (the problem corner) — low outcomes despite high spend, a sign of inefficiency.
eu_compare_scatter_health_explainer: Government health spend (% of GDP) against life expectancy at birth. Bulgaria allocates a noticeably smaller share of its economy to health than the EU average, and at the same time citizens live meaningfully fewer years. The combination — low spend and low outcome — points to both underfunding and questions about how efficiently the money is used.
eu_compare_scatter_social_title: Social protection: spend vs poverty risk
eu_compare_scatter_social_explainer: Government social-protection spend (% of GDP) against the AROPE index — the share of population at risk of poverty or social exclusion. Bulgaria allocates a meaningfully smaller share of its economy to social protection than the EU27 average (below most peers as well), and at the same time records the EU's highest poverty risk.
eu_compare_sources_eurostat: Eurostat — macro and fiscal indicators (gov_10a_main, gov_10q_ggdebt, namq_10_gdp, prc_hicp_minr, une_rt_q and others). Fetched: {{date}}.
eu_compare_sources_silc: Eurostat SILC — inequality and poverty (ilc_di12, ilc_di11, ilc_peps01n) + demo_mlexpec for life expectancy. Fetched: {{date}}.
eu_compare_sources_cofog: Eurostat gov_10a_exp — functional classification of government expenditure (COFOG). Fetched: {{date}}.
eu_compare_sources_wgi: World Bank — Worldwide Governance Indicators (source 3, GOV_WGI_*.EST). Fetched: {{date}}.
eu_compare_sources_eu27_synth: The EU27 average on the WGI radar is an unweighted mean across the 27 member states — the World Bank does not publish a regional aggregate.
cabinet_score_row_heading: By cabinet — averages over the term
cabinet_selector_hint: Click a pill above to see that cabinet's term-average indicators.
demographics_description: Bulgaria's 2021 census mapped onto our regional electoral data — ethnicity, religion, education and age structure side by side with vote shares.
demographics_explainer: All figures come from the National Statistical Institute's 2021 Population and Housing Census. Use the controls below to compare oblasts, see per-region breakdowns on the map, or correlate any demographic with a party's municipal vote share.
demographics_regions_description: Census 2021 demographics for Bulgaria's 28 administrative oblasts — sortable by every column.
demographics_municipalities_description: Census 2021 demographics for Bulgaria's 265 municipalities — sortable, filterable by name, paginated 30 per page.
census_crosstab_explainer: Each bubble is one municipality. Pick a party and a demographic dimension to see how the two correlate across all 265 municipalities. Sofia city is shown as a single bubble.
census_correlation_positive: {{party}} tends to overperform in municipalities with a higher {{metric}} (r = {{r}}).
census_correlation_negative: {{party}} tends to underperform in municipalities with a higher {{metric}} (r = {{r}}).
census_scatter_note: Vote share by municipality for the {{date}} election; demographics from the 2021 census.
census_tooltip_click_hint: Click to open the municipality page
census_tile_hint: Snapshot from the National Statistical Institute's 2021 Population and Housing Census ({{date}}).
census_settlement_dimensions_note: Ethnicity, religion and education are only published at the municipality level by NSI; settlements show population, age and sex.
regional_tile_hint: Latest annual values per oblast (Eurostat NUTS 3 + Агенция по заетостта) with year-over-year change.
regional_sofia_oblast_footnote: Eurostat publishes Sofia city as one NUTS 3 entity — the same value applies to all three Sofia electoral districts.
regional_source_attribution: Sources: Eurostat, NSI and the Employment Agency.
indicators_tile_hint: Latest annual values for the municipality with year-over-year change.
indicators_sofia_city_footnote: Sofia city is published as a single value; the same number applies to all 24 city districts.
indicators_map_explainer: Latest annual values per municipality. Click a municipality to drill down. Sofia city is published as one value, so all 24 districts share the same number.
regional_map_explainer: Latest annual values per oblast — Eurostat NUTS 3 indicators, НСИ open data and long-term unemployment from Агенция по заетостта. Click an oblast to drill down. Sofia city (BG411) is published as one entity, so all three Sofia electoral districts share the same value.
bnb_fdi_source: Bulgarian National Bank — foreign direct investment in Bulgaria (balance of payments, BPM6); the monthly net-flow-by-investment-type export (total, equity, reinvested earnings, debt instruments) back to 2010. Surfaced on /indicators/fiscal as the monthly FDI tile
eurostat_macro_source: Eurostat — macroeconomic and social indicators (GDP, HICP, unemployment, fiscal balances, house-price index, FDI flows BPM6, intentional-homicide rate per 100K, prisoners per 100K); fetched for Bulgaria and for the EU27 aggregate + Romania, Greece, Hungary and Croatia. Surfaced on /indicators as a KPI dashboard + four domain sub-pages (economy, fiscal, governance, society) plus a peer-explorer at /indicators/compare
eurostat_regional_source: Eurostat — NUTS 3 (per-oblast) indicators: GDP per capita, population, net migration, recorded-theft rate and active-enterprise density
indicators_nsi_opendata_source: Национален статистически институт — open-data (JSON-stat) per-oblast cross-sections: cumulative FDI per capita, museum visits and hospital beds
eurostat_cofog_source: Eurostat gov_10a_exp — general-government expenditure by COFOG-99 function: the ten top-level functions (general services, defence, health, education, social protection, etc.) as % of GDP. Powers the functional-classification tile on /budget, the "what did your taxes buy" calculator, and the side-by-side per-country stacked bars on /indicators/compare
admin_egov_source: Eurostat isoc_ciegi_ac — e-government activities of individuals (indicator I_IUGOV1, interaction with public authorities in the last 12 months, % of individuals). Powers the digital-government tile on /sector/administration — Bulgaria's e-service use vs the EU average and peers.
admin_digital_skills_source: Eurostat isoc_sk_dskl_i21 — citizen digital skills (Digital Skills Indicator 2.0, the DESI human-capital pillar): share with at least basic skills across the five areas, by wave 2021/2023/2025, plus the full 27-member youth (16-24) cross-section. Powers the citizen digital-skills band on /sector/administration and the EU choropleth.
eurostat_silc_source: Eurostat SILC — income and living conditions: Gini coefficient (ilc_di12), S80/S20 income quintile share ratio (ilc_di11), AROPE — at-risk-of-poverty-or-social-exclusion rate (ilc_peps01n), overall poverty risk (ilc_li02). Powers the inequality panel + spend-vs-outcome scatters on /indicators/compare
eurostat_life_expectancy_source: Eurostat demo_mlexpec — life expectancy at birth by sex and age. Powers the "health spend vs life expectancy" scatter on /indicators/compare
eurostat_tourism_source: Eurostat tour_occ_nim / tour_occ_ninraw — nights spent by month and by country of origin. Powers the seasonality and source-market tiles on /sector/tourism
social_asp_source: Agency for Social Assistance (АСП) — the annual activity report (asp.government.bg). National annual data on benefits by type: monthly child allowances, disability support, targeted heating aid, guaranteed minimum income — recipients/cases and amount paid. Powers the /sector/social view (no per-oblast breakdown is published). data/social/benefits.json
social_poverty_source: Eurostat ilc_li10 / ilc_li02 — at-risk-of-poverty before and after social transfers (pensions excluded). Shows how much poverty transfers remove — in Bulgaria ~27% vs ~33% EU average. Powers the “Social transfers and poverty” tile on /sector/social. data/social/poverty_impact.json
prices_kzp_source: CPC «How much does it cost» — daily retail price open data (101-product basket, per settlement; kolkostruva.bg). Powers the Consumption (cost-of-living) view and the price tiles on the place dashboards
prices_eurostat_pli_source: Eurostat — food price level indices (prc_ppp_ind_1, EU=100). The official Bulgaria-vs-EU food price comparison on /consumption/eu
prices_oil_bulletin_source: European Commission — Weekly Oil Bulletin. Consumer fuel prices (petrol 95 & diesel, incl. VAT) in Bulgaria vs the EU average on /consumption/fuel
sovereign_debt_bnb_source: Bulgarian National Bank — Fiscal Agent auction archive (domestic ДЦК, 2019+)
sovereign_debt_luxse_source: Luxembourg Stock Exchange — Republic of Bulgaria Eurobond listings (since 2002)
sovereign_debt_minfin_source: Bulgarian Ministry of Finance — monthly debt bulletin + Eurobond press releases
governance_ti_cpi_source: Transparency International — Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI), Bulgaria
governance_wb_wgi_source: World Bank — Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI), Bulgaria. /governments shows Rule of Law, Control of Corruption and Government Effectiveness; /indicators/compare draws all six dimensions (plus Voice & Accountability, Political Stability, Regulatory Quality) as a radar for Bulgaria and the peer set. The EU27 mean is an unweighted average computed locally — the World Bank does not publish a regional aggregate
governance_eurobarometer_source: Standard Eurobarometer — "tend to trust" share for parliament, government and the EU (Bulgaria); annual mean of spring + autumn waves. Powers the trust series on /indicators/governance
indicators_az_source: Агенция по заетостта — registered unemployment per municipality (annual review, XLSX)
indicators_mon_dzi_source: Министерство на образованието via data.egov.bg — state-matura (DZI) results per school, aggregated to municipality
indicators_nsi_pop_source: Национален статистически институт — annual population per municipality (Pop_6.1.1 timeseries XLSX)
indicators_nsi_vital_source: Национален статистически институт — annual births, deaths and internal migration per municipality (timeseries XLSX) — feeds natural population change + net migration
indicators_nsi_landuse_source: Национален статистически институт — annual "Land use distribution of the Republic of Bulgaria" press-release annex, computed from АГКК's digital cadastral map. 28 districts × 8 land-use categories (urbanized, transport, agricultural, forest, water, protected, disturbed, unclassified) — drives the My-Area "Property stock" tile.
indicators_grao_source: ГРАО — settlement-level population by permanent and current address (quarterly t41nm tables)
procurement_aop_source: Агенция за обществени поръчки (АОП) via data.egov.bg — fortnightly OCDS-standard contract bundles (since 2026) and annual CSV dumps for prior years (2011-2023)
procurement_aop_debarred_source: АОП "Стопански субекти с нарушения" register (www2.aop.bg) — debarred-suppliers list, refreshed when КЗК rulings become final. Drives the "debarred" risk-flag chip on contract rows.
procurement_eop_source: ЦАИС ЕОП open data (storage.eop.bg) — the e-procurement platform's own daily flat "договори" feed, used to gap-fill ~900 small contracting authorities (mostly schools and kindergartens) that АОП's OCDS export omits.
procurement_kzk_source: КЗК public register (reg.cpc.bg) — every appeal against a public-procurement procedure with its УНП, status and merits outcome, joined to the tender by exact procedure number. Manual headed-browser ingest (the register is geo-gated).
eu_funds_isun_source: ИСУН 2020 public module (2020.eufunds.bg) — Beneficiary register. The EU-funds Management & Monitoring Information System lists every organisation that has signed an EU-funds contract (2014-2020, 2021-2027 and the Recovery Plan), with contracts signed, funds contracted and funds actually paid.
eu_funds_isun_projects_source: ИСУН 2020 public module (2020.eufunds.bg) — Project register. One row per signed contract with the programme, beneficiary, total / grant / paid amount, status and implementation location (Местонахождение, mostly settlement-level), used to attribute EU-funds spending to specific settlements and municipalities.
eu_funds_political_join_source: Political-economy cross-reference (officials × EU-funds beneficiaries) — joins ИСУН 2020 against Сметна палата declarations + the Commerce Registry for sitting MPs, cabinet members, deputy ministers, state-agency heads, regional governors, mayors, deputy mayors, council chairs, councillors, and chief architects. Also overlaid with the public-procurement (АОП) register for award overlap and the АОП debarred-suppliers list.
funds_index_intro: Every organisation that has signed an EU-funds contract recorded in ИСУН 2020 — the 2014-2020 and 2021-2027 programmes plus the Recovery Plan.
funds_index_beneficiaries_hint: Organisations with at least one EU-funds contract, as listed in the ИСУН register (a parent and its sub-units count separately).
funds_index_contracted_hint: Total value of signed EU-funds contracts (Договорени средства).
funds_index_paid_hint: Total actually disbursed to beneficiaries (Реално изплатени суми).
funds_index_mp_hint: MPs whose declared business interests intersect EU-funds beneficiaries.
funds_index_source_hint: Source: ИСУН 2020 public beneficiary register. “Contracted funds” is the total contracted project value per ИСУН — for some programmes it includes the beneficiary’s own co-financing, not only the EU grant.
funds_mp_intro: Beneficiary companies in which a sitting or former MP has a declared ownership stake or a Commerce Registry management role.
funds_mp_source_hint: MP linkages from Court-of-Audit declarations and Commerce Registry filings. A connection describes what the MP is on record for — not in itself an accusation of wrongdoing.
dual_corpus_subtitle: Firms that both won public-procurement contracts and drew EU funds.
integrity_page_description: Programme-level concentration, serial winners, and АОП debarred-supplier overlap on EU-funds beneficiaries.
